name: ISO/IEC 27001
description: The international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). ISO 27001:2022 introduced a complete restructuring of Annex A controls — from 114 controls across 14 clauses to 93 controls across 4 themes — with 11 new controls addressing cloud security, threat intelligence, and data masking.

highlights: Annex A completely restructured: 114 controls in 14 clauses → 93 controls across 4 themes (Organizational, People, Physical, Technological)
11 brand-new controls added covering threat intelligence, cloud security, ICT readiness, data masking, web filtering, secure coding, and configuration management
Controls reduced from 114 to 93 through consolidation — no controls were deleted, they were merged or reorganized
New 'attributes' system introduced: each control now has 5 attribute tags (#concept, #control_type, #cybersecurity_concepts, #operational_capabilities, #security_domains)
Main body clauses 4-10 updated to align with ISO harmonized structure (Annex SL), making integration with ISO 9001, 27701 and other ISO standards easier
Transition period ended October 31, 2025 — all certificates must now be to ISO 27001:2022

changes.title: Annex A restructured from 14 clauses to 4 themes
changes.rationale: The 14-clause structure of ISO 27001:2013 was based on the original BS 7799 organization. The 2022 revision reorganized all controls into 4 logical themes: Organizational controls (37), People controls (8), Physical controls (14), and Technological controls (34). No controls were deleted — all 114 were either carried forward, merged, or consolidated.

changes.title: 5.7: Threat intelligence (new)
changes.rationale: Threat intelligence was entirely absent from ISO 27001:2013. Control 5.7 requires organizations to collect, analyze, and share information about information security threats. This is one of the most significant additions, reflecting the maturation of threat intelligence as a discipline since 2013.

changes.title: 5.23: Information security for use of cloud services (new)
changes.rationale: Cloud was nascent in 2013 and not explicitly addressed. Control 5.23 requires organizations to establish processes for acquisition, use, management, and exit from cloud services — directly addressing the cloud security gap in the prior version.

changes.title: 5.30: ICT readiness for business continuity (new)
changes.rationale: Carving out ICT readiness as a distinct control from general business continuity reflects post-COVID and pandemic-preparedness awareness. Organizations must now specifically plan for ICT disruption scenarios, not just general business continuity.

changes.title: 7.4: Physical security monitoring (new)
changes.rationale: Physical security monitoring (CCTV, alarm systems, access logs) was previously implied rather than explicit. Control 7.4 makes physical premises monitoring an explicit requirement, closing a gap common in 2013 certification audits.

changes.title: 8.9: Configuration management (new)
changes.rationale: Configuration management was buried in other controls in 2013. Control 8.9 makes it explicit: configurations of hardware, software, services, and networks must be documented, implemented, monitored, and reviewed. This reflects the importance of configuration drift prevention in cloud and hybrid environments.

changes.title: 8.10: Information deletion (new)
changes.rationale: Data deletion and the right to erasure — driven by GDPR and global privacy regulations — required an explicit control. 8.10 requires information to be deleted when no longer needed, across storage devices and services.

changes.title: 8.11: Data masking (new)
changes.rationale: Data masking, pseudonymization, and anonymization were not explicitly addressed in 2013. Control 8.11 introduces these as explicit techniques for protecting personal and sensitive data, aligning with GDPR's privacy-by-design principles.

changes.title: 8.12: Data leakage prevention (new)
changes.rationale: DLP capabilities were mature by 2022 but absent from 2013. Control 8.12 requires DLP measures to be applied to systems, networks, and output devices that process, store, or transmit sensitive information.

changes.title: 8.16: Monitoring activities (new)
changes.rationale: Monitoring was scattered across multiple 2013 controls. Control 8.16 consolidates continuous monitoring into a dedicated control requiring detection of anomalous behavior in networks, systems, and applications.

changes.title: 8.23: Web filtering (new)
changes.rationale: Web filtering to prevent malicious content access was not explicitly required in 2013. Control 8.23 makes this an explicit control, covering access to external websites and protecting users from malware, phishing, and unauthorized sites.

changes.title: 8.28: Secure coding (new)
changes.rationale: Secure coding principles were implied in 2013's system development controls but not explicit. Control 8.28 makes secure coding an explicit requirement covering principles, guidelines, and techniques — aligning ISO 27001 with OWASP, NIST SSDF, and software security best practices.

changes.title: A.9.1.1 + A.9.1.2 merged into 5.15 (Access control)
changes.rationale: Access control policy (A.9.1.1) and access to networks and network services (A.9.1.2) were merged into a single control 5.15. The merged control covers the full access control lifecycle under one umbrella.

changes.title: Information access restriction + secure logon merged into 8.2 (Privileged access rights)
changes.rationale: Access restriction (A.9.4.1) and secure log-on procedures (A.9.4.5) were merged and consolidated. The 2022 version more clearly addresses privileged access as a distinct security concern.

changes.title: Operational procedures, change management, separation merged into 5.37
changes.rationale: Three separate 2013 controls covering documented procedures (A.12.1.1), change management (A.12.1.2), and separation of development/test/production (A.12.1.4) were merged into a single documented operating procedures control 5.37.

changes.title: Software installation + change control procedures merged into 8.32
changes.rationale: Software installation on operational systems and system change control procedures were merged into a unified change management control for ICT systems (8.32).

changes.title: New attributes system added to all controls
changes.rationale: ISO 27001:2022 introduced a new 'attributes' classification system. Each of the 93 controls is tagged with 5 attribute types: #control_type (preventive/detective/corrective), #information_security_properties (C/I/A), #cybersecurity_concepts (NIST CSF alignment: identify/protect/detect/respond/recover), #operational_capabilities (functional category), and #security_domains (governance/asset/protection/defence/resilience). This enables risk-based filtering and cross-standard mapping.

changes.title: Statement of Applicability (SoA) requirements unchanged but Annex A reference updated
changes.rationale: The SoA requirements are unchanged, but because the control set changed from 114 to 93 controls (renumbered), all existing SoAs required a remapping exercise. Organizations had until October 31, 2025 to complete transition certification audits.

changes.title: Clause 4.2 — Understanding needs of interested parties, note added
changes.rationale: A note was added to Clause 4.2 clarifying that 'interested parties' can have requirements that go beyond legal/regulatory/contractual — explicitly including customer security expectations and internal stakeholder needs. This aligns with the ISO harmonized structure.

changes.title: Clause 6.1.2 — Risk assessment process now explicitly requires criteria for accepting risk
changes.rationale: The risk assessment process clause was updated to require explicit criteria for accepting risks, not just identifying them. This closes a gap where organizations performed risk assessments but had no formal process for determining which risks to accept vs. treat.

changes.title: Clause 9.1 — Performance evaluation method explicitly required
changes.rationale: Clause 9.1 was updated to require organizations to explicitly determine when and how performance evaluation methods will be analyzed and evaluated — not just what to measure. This pushes organizations toward defined measurement cadences.

changes.title: Clause 10 reordered: Continual Improvement now before Nonconformity
changes.rationale: In 2013, Clause 10.1 covered nonconformity and corrective action, and 10.2 covered continual improvement. In 2022, the order flipped: 10.1 is continual improvement and 10.2 is nonconformity. This aligns with the ISO harmonized structure and signals a cultural shift toward proactive improvement over reactive correction.

changes.title: Policies for information security merged and updated
changes.rationale: The 2013 controls A.5.1.1 (Policies for information security) and A.5.1.2 (Review of policies) were merged into a single control 5.1. The merged control now explicitly requires policies to be appropriate to the organization AND communicated and available to all relevant parties.

changes.title: Supplier relationships — information security in supplier agreements updated
changes.rationale: The 2022 control 5.19 (Information security in supplier relationships) is significantly expanded from A.15.1.1. It now explicitly covers the full supplier lifecycle, type-specific requirements, and cloud services — aligning with the new control 5.23 for cloud services.

changes.title: Business continuity controls reorganized into 5.29 and 5.30
changes.rationale: Four separate 2013 business continuity controls were restructured into two: 5.29 (Information security during disruption) and the entirely new 5.30 (ICT readiness for business continuity). This reflects a clearer separation between general BC planning and specific ICT continuity planning.

changes.title: Control count: 114 → 93 (through merging, not deletion)
changes.rationale: The reduction from 114 to 93 controls is entirely the result of merging redundant or closely related controls. No control requirement was eliminated. The reduction makes the standard less repetitive and easier to audit, while the 11 new controls bring it current with the 2022 threat landscape.

highlights: Amendment 1:2024 ("Climate action changes") published February 2024 — two textual additions, no changes to Annex A controls
Clause 4.1: organizations must determine whether climate change is a relevant ISMS issue; Clause 4.2: note that interested parties may have climate-related requirements
Certification impact is minimal: typically addressed with a documented context-review determination

changes.title: Clause 4.1 — organizations must determine whether climate change is a relevant issue
changes.rationale: Amendment 1 adds a requirement to Clause 4.1 (Understanding the organization and its context): the organization must determine whether climate change is a relevant internal or external issue for the ISMS. It does not mandate that climate change IS relevant — only that the question is considered and the determination documented. Full amendment text available from ISO (copyrighted).

changes.title: Clause 4.2 — new note on interested parties' climate-related requirements
changes.rationale: Amendment 1 adds a note to Clause 4.2 (Understanding the needs and expectations of interested parties): relevant interested parties can have requirements related to climate change. Added across ISO management system standards per the IAF/ISO Joint Communiqué of February 23, 2024, reflecting ISO's London Declaration climate commitments. Annex A controls are unchanged.
